paper_title: Breaking the Curse of Kernelization: Budgeted Stochastic Gradient Descent for Large-Scale SVM Training
core_contribution: The paper proposes Budgeted Stochastic Gradient Descent (BSGD) algorithms for large-scale kernel SVM training that maintain constant space and time complexity by limiting the number of support vectors through budget maintenance strategies. The method addresses the curse of kernelization where the number of support vectors grows unboundedly with data size, making standard kernel SGD impractical for large datasets.
algorithm_steps: Step 1: Initialize budget B (maximum number of support vectors allowed)
Step 2: Receive a new training example (x_t, y_t)
Step 3: Compute prediction using current support vectors
Step 4: Update support vector weights using SGD gradient step
Step 5: If number of support vectors exceeds budget B, apply budget maintenance strategy (removal, projection, or merging)
Step 6: Return to step 2 for next example
key_equations: SGD update rule: gradient computation for hinge loss with respect to support vector weights
Budget maintenance: optimal removal strategy removes support vector with smallest weight
Projection strategy: minimizes accumulated loss when projecting one support vector onto remaining ones
Merging strategy: for Gaussian kernels, new support vector lies on line connecting merged support vectors

key_assumptions.assumption: Fixed budget size B is sufficient to capture the essential structure of the data
key_assumptions.why_needed: The method requires limiting support vectors to achieve constant space/time complexity
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When the true decision boundary requires more than B support vectors to represent accurately

key_assumptions.assumption: Budget maintenance strategies preserve sufficient information about discarded support vectors
key_assumptions.why_needed: To minimize the gap between BSGD and optimal SVM solutions
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When removed/merged support vectors contain critical boundary information that cannot be preserved

key_assumptions.assumption: Training examples arrive in a reasonable order (not adversarially ordered)
key_assumptions.why_needed: SGD convergence properties depend on example ordering
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When examples are ordered to maximize interference with budget maintenance

baseline_limitations_identified: Existing kernel SVM methods suffer from unbounded growth in model size and update time with data size, making them impractical for large-scale learning
proposed_improvement: BSGD maintains constant space and time complexity through budget maintenance while preserving accuracy close to optimal SVM solutions
condition_where_baseline_wins: When computational resources are unlimited and the dataset size is small enough that the full kernel SVM can be trained efficiently without budget constraints
toy_dataset_justification: These datasets allow testing of nonlinear classification capabilities with known ground truth, varying levels of noise, and different degrees of class overlap to evaluate budget maintenance strategies
reproducibility_notes: Algorithm details are provided but implementation of budget maintenance strategies requires careful attention to computational efficiency and numerical stability

key_components_for_ablation.component: Budget maintenance strategy (removal vs projection vs merging)
key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Determines how to handle support vector overflow when budget is exceeded
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Without budget maintenance, algorithm reverts to standard SGD with unbounded growth

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Controls the trade-off between computational efficiency and model accuracy
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Smaller budgets increase efficiency but may hurt accuracy; larger budgets improve accuracy but reduce efficiency gains

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Determines the feature space and affects budget maintenance strategies
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Linear kernel eliminates the curse of kernelization but reduces nonlinear modeling capability

known_failure_modes.scenario: When the optimal decision boundary requires more support vectors than the budget allows
known_failure_modes.reason: Budget constraint forces loss of critical boundary information
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Fixed budget size B is sufficient to capture the essential structure of the data

known_failure_modes.scenario: When training data has high noise levels or many outliers
known_failure_modes.reason: Budget maintenance may remove important boundary examples or retain noisy ones
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Budget maintenance strategies preserve sufficient information about discarded support vectors

known_failure_modes.reason: Budget allocation may not properly represent minority class boundaries
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Training examples arrive in a reasonable order
